aliases: Grilled chicken meatball with tare sauce

english: Grilled chicken meatball with tare sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for yakitori tsukune (Japanese grilled chicken ball). Mapped to the broad chicken meat food product class as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for skewered or grilled minced chicken preparations is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for yakitori tsukune. A00GJ (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. The glazed, minced, and skewered preparation specifics are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for yakitori tsukune or Japanese grilled chicken ball as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of yakitori tsukune is two skewers, each holding two to three chicken balls, totalling approximately 100 g, as commonly served at Japanese yakitori restaurants and izakayas.

nutrient_source.title: Yakitori (Chicken) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for yakitori chicken ball (F11020055) used as the primary nutrient source. Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are taken directly from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked ground chicken (NDB No. 05064) proportioned to the HPB total fat value of 13.4 g/100 g; saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.8 g/100 g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrients beyond calcium and iron are set to -1 as they are not reported in the HPB record and detailed fatty acid or micronutrient data for this specific preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yakitori tsukune in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 45 (low) by analogy with mixed meat-and-sauce preparations with moderate sugar content (tare glaze contains mirin and sugar). The analogous reference is soy-glazed chicken preparations with similar carbohydrate composition (~10 g/100 g), which cluster in the GI 40–50 range in the University of Sydney database. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (45 × 10.4) / 100 = 4.7 g, classified as low. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of yakitori tsukune is recommended for higher-confidence classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 521 mg per 100 g, substantially exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit yakitori tsukune to small portions (50–60 g) and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as the tare glaze contributes the majority of the 521 mg/100 g sodium load; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato may partially offset the sodium effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (10.4 g/100 g), high protein (18.8 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~45) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yakitori tsukune is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions; the low carbohydrate load (glycaemic load ~4.7 per 100 g serving) and high protein content support stable postprandial glucose, but the tare glaze sugar (4.4 g/100 g) should be monitored and sauce quantity limited.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (521 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is moderate at approximately 3.8 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, yakitori tsukune should be consumed in small portions due to its high sodium content (521 mg/100 g), which contributes to blood pressure elevation; the estimated saturated fat of 3.8 g/100 g is below the high-concern threshold but should be considered within total daily saturated fat limits, and grilled preparation is preferable to fried alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ginger ingredient partially offset by high sodium (521 mg/100 g) which may exacerbate tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger in yakitori tsukune provides gingerols with anti-inflammatory properties beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis, and grilling avoids AGE formation; however, the high sodium content (521 mg/100 g) may worsen RA-related oedema and should be minimised by requesting reduced-soy-sauce preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yakitori tsukune (焼き鳥つくね) is a Japanese grilled chicken ball made from seasoned ground chicken mixed with ginger, spring onion, and egg, shaped into balls or oval patties, skewered, and grilled over charcoal or a gas flame. The skewers are basted repeatedly with tare — a sweet-savoury glaze of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — which caramelises on the surface during grilling to produce a glossy, slightly sticky coating with a deep umami flavour. The texture is tender and juicy inside with a lightly charred exterior. Tsukune is a staple of Japanese yakitori restaurants and izakayas, typically served two to three balls per skewer alongside cold beer or sake. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 237 kcal, 18.8 g protein, 13.4 g fat, and 10.4 g carbohydrate, with 521 mg sodium from the tare glaze. The high protein content makes it suitable for muscle maintenance, while the low carbohydrate load (glycaemic load ~4.7) supports glycaemic control for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The primary clinical concern is the elevated sodium content, which makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension unless portion size is restricted and reduced-soy-sauce preparation is requested. Calcium (69 mg/100 g) and iron (1.2 mg/100 g) contribute modestly to micronutrient intake. The grilled preparation avoids the advanced glycation end-products associated with deep-frying.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11020055 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked ground chicken data proportioned to the HPB total fat value. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for soy-glazed chicken preparations. All mandatory Japanese-language naming is present. Ontology codes are defensible broad matches with documented gap notes.
notes: Fields set to -1 (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel beyond calcium and iron, and moisture data for yakitori tsukune are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11020055, and no exact match for this specific preparation exists in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05064 (chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted) proportioned to HPB total fat of 13.4 g/100 g; this is an approximation and direct measurement is recommended. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of yakitori tsukune is recommended. Input cultural tags 'western' and 'plant_based' have been corrected: this is a Japanese dish and is not plant-based. Halal status is marked false in dietary_flags as traditional yakitori tare may contain mirin (alcohol-containing ingredient); halal-certified variants exist but cannot be assumed for the generic record.
